title: Naming It Out Loud: The Practice of Articulation As Somatic Liberation
dek: You carry fury in your masseter and trapezius, clenched tight when you were told anger was ugly. Naming your experience aloud releases tension stored in your neck and jaw, rewiring the body’s memory of silence. Inspired by the work of Karla McLaren and Resmaa Menakem, this practice is a reckoning with the body's biology—not pathology—to reclaim your voice through precise, embodied articulation.
bodyMarkdown:
## Naming It Out Loud: The Practice of Articulation  
### The Physical Sensation of Holding Words Back  

It’s late at night, just past midnight, and the house is wrapped in a hush that feels like a weight pressing against your chest. You’re lying awake, the muscles along your throat so tight it’s as if a thin wire is wrapped around your larynx, pulling your voice deeper into shadow. Your jaw clenches—masseter muscle taut, the fascia thickened and unwilling to release. This is the body’s secret language, the biology of silence that no one taught you to name.  

You were raised to believe that fury, sharp and raw, was ugly. But the body remembers every word you swallowed whole. The tightness in your throat is not just metaphor; it’s a physical reality. The masseter, that thick muscle running from your cheekbone to your jaw, contracts reflexively when words are unsaid. There’s a hardness there, like the bark of a tree hardened by years of storm. When you hold back a name for what you feel, when you tuck your rage into the corners of your mouth, your body responds with this tension, this pressure, this refusal to relax.  

Thich Nhat Hanh calls this “the cloud of unspoken emotions,” and you can feel it in the way your diaphragm hesitates, the breath caught halfway between your ribs. It’s not just silence; it’s an embodied contraction of the nervous system. The jaw remembers. The throat remembers. The voice, that thing so intimately tied to the self, gets buried beneath layers of tension and fear. You can almost taste the metallic staleness of held-back words, like copper on your tongue in the stillness of the night.  

This biological response is not pathology; it’s survival. Gabor Maté speaks about how the body archives trauma in the fascia and muscle, a ledger of every moment you were told to be quiet, to “tone it down,” to make yourself smaller. Your trapezius muscle tightens, spanning from the base of your skull to the middle of your back, as if bracing against a storm. Your levator scapulae, those muscles that lift the shoulder blades, knot in quiet protest. All of these muscles become soldiers holding the line against expression, guarding the silence you were taught to carry.  

You might feel the tension shift as the night deepens, the muscles tightening in waves, like a tide you cannot control. This is not weakness. This is biology. The body knows. It is recording the moment you hesitate, when the words hover on your tongue but never leap out. Naming it out loud—the practice of articulation—is the first step towards releasing this tension, towards telling the jaw it can soften, the throat it can open, the voice it can rise.  

The night holds its breath with you. The body waits for the sound.

## The Anatomy of Unspoken Anger: How Your Body Holds What You Don’t Say

Late at night, when the world softens into shadows and quiet, you might notice a tightness just beneath your collarbone, where the levator scapulae muscle hooks between your neck and shoulder. It’s subtle, maybe a dull ache, but persistent. This is no accident. It’s the physical echo of words you never spoke, anger you didn’t let out, and fury that was deemed “unladylike” or “too much.” The body remembers what the mind disowns, and it does so through the fascia, the diaphragm, and muscles like the levator scapulae—an intricate network that holds your unexpressed feelings in place.

Fascia, the connective tissue that envelops your muscles, bones, and organs, is more than just biological packing material. It’s a living web where your emotions lodge when you don’t give them voice. Imagine the fascia around your ribcage tightening like a corset, constricting not only breath but your capacity to articulate dissatisfaction or rage. Gabor Maté, whose work on trauma and emotion bridges the somatic with the psychological, reminds us that this tightening is no mere metaphor. It’s a physical cost: chronic tension in the diaphragm, the muscle that breathes life into your words, diminishes not just your breath but your ability to express yourself fully.

When anger is not named, it doesn’t disappear. Instead, it curls into the diaphragm, the very muscle that lifts and falls with your breath, clamping down like a clenched fist on your ability to speak. The diaphragm’s restrictive hold is often why you feel “choked up” or “like you have a lump in your throat.” This is the body’s way of saying, “You are holding back.” This isn’t weakness or failure. It’s biology responding to the social injunction that your fury must be hidden. Pema Chödrön’s teachings about leaning into discomfort echo here: your diaphragm’s tightness is an invitation to meet your anger, not run from it.

Further down the chain, the levator scapulae stiffens in patterns shaped by your history of silencing. This muscle, responsible for lifting the scapula and stabilizing the neck, often tightens when you are tense or anxious, but also when you suppress irritation and frustration. The fascia around it becomes less pliable, the muscle itself a clenched fist under skin. You may catch yourself grinding your teeth at night, the masseter muscle working overtime—another physical marker of unexpressed fury. The jaw remembers what the voice refuses to say.

Understanding this anatomy, this embodied map of unsaid words, is the first step to reclaiming your voice. It’s how you start to untangle the fascia, soften the diaphragm, and release the levator scapulae’s grip. This is not pathology; this is biology. It is the body’s diligent, if painful, record of what you were taught to keep silent. Gabor Maté’s insights remind us: hidden emotions don’t vanish; they embed themselves in muscle and fascia, demanding acknowledgment before they can let go. Your body is calling you to name your anger out loud—not to explode, but to articulate, to breathe, to heal.

## Naming It Out Loud: The Practice of Articulation

You’ve carried it for so long—the tight coil in the masseter muscle, clenched jaw trembling with unspoken words. The fury you learned was ugly, something to swallow whole or hide beneath a polite smile. But here, in the quiet that lingers after midnight, when the house is still and your body aches with the remembered weight of silence, you try something different. You name it out loud.

This isn’t about yelling at someone else or lashing out in a moment of chaos. It’s about the deliberate, slow articulation of what roils beneath your ribs, the visceral truth that the diaphragm resists holding back. You lean into the edge of the chair, or maybe you stand barefoot on the cold wooden floor, and you voice the sensation—a low, steady growl or a clear, clipped phrase that names the exact shape of your anger. “I’m furious that I was dismissed.” “I feel trapped and unheard.” “This injustice burns in my chest.” The air shifts as the muscles along your throat, the sternocleidomastoid and the delicate fascia around your larynx, begin to loosen.

What you’re doing here is far from trivial. Robert Augustus Masters teaches that the body remembers, that the jaw remembers. That tight masseter has been clenched in service of “keeping the peace” for years, refusing to open because you were taught that your anger is dangerous or shameful. But when you speak your anger aloud, even softly, it sends a message to your nervous system: this feeling is valid. This sensation has a name. It is allowed to exist. The trapezius and levator scapulae, those chronic tension holders, begin to sigh into release as you relinquish the burden of silence.

You may notice the subtle shift in your breath, how the diaphragm, usually held rigid by fear or repression, softens and expands. It’s a subtle unfolding, like the first light of dawn stretching over a cold winter’s morning. Your throat might feel raw, your voice a little cracked, but this is not damage—it’s biology waking up. This is your nervous system recalibrating, no longer needing to constrict around a secret it can now articulate.

In this practice, there is no rush. Pema Chödrön reminds us that anger, like all emotions, is a visitor. Naming it out loud is the act of inviting it into the room, offering it a cup of tea instead of slamming the door. You don’t have to justify or explain. You don’t have to frame it for anyone else’s comfort. This is your body speaking its truth, and you are the honored witness.

You may find that the simple act of naming the anger aloud becomes a ritual, a somatic medicine as potent as any herbal infusion. The scent of your breath mingles with the faint herbal notes of peppermint or chamomile you’ve steeped nearby, grounding your practice in the earth’s fierce kindness. This is not about fixing or silencing your fury. It’s about giving it a voice, freeing the diaphragm, unhinging the jaw, and letting the tension in the shoulders dissolve into the night air.

In the quiet aftermath, when the body softens and the breath deepens, you will feel the difference. The anger no longer a snarling beast trapped in the throat, but a named presence, held and held with respect. The body knows this is not pathology. This is biology. This is the beginning of reclaiming your voice.

## Herbal Support for the Throat and Voice: Speaking Your Fury Into Being

It’s just past 3 a.m., and the house is heavy with the damp weight of spring rain. You lie awake, the ache in your throat not just a symptom but a signal—a tightening in the cricothyroid muscle, that thin band that tweaks the pitch of your voice, now stiff and reluctant. The masseter, your jaw muscle, clenches in silent resistance, holding back the words you were never taught to say out loud. This is where your body remembers what your mind tries to forget: fury is not ugly; it is your truth demanding passage.

When you speak your fury, you engage more than just the vocal cords. The entire throat complex—the larynx, the pharynx, the intricate web of fascia and muscles—must soften, open, and strengthen. Here is where herbal allies meet the architecture of your voice, supporting both the physical and the nervous system that governs it.

Elecampane root (Inula helenium) is a teacher in this regard. It carries a resinous, earthy aroma that smells like old forests and wet stone. Elecampane is a demulcent and expectorant herb: it soothes the mucous membranes lining your throat and lungs while gently clearing out congestion, allowing your voice to rise unburdened. The plant’s bitter compounds stimulate digestion too—because speaking your truth is as much about internal processing as external expression. When you sip a warm infusion of elecampane in the late afternoon, you can feel the herb’s glycerin-like slipperiness coating your pharynx, softening the constricting fascia surrounding your larynx. It’s a slow uncoiling, a permission granted to your voice to unfold.

Licorice root (Glycyrrhiza glabra) offers something different but equally vital. Its sweetness is thick and almost caramel-like, lingering on the tongue and throat. Licorice is an adaptogen with a long history in Traditional Chinese Medicine for harmonizing and strengthening the middle burner—the energetic center that includes the throat. It modulates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, which means it helps regulate your stress response. When the levator scapulae muscles at the back of your neck seize in tension—common when you hold back rage—licorice’s anti-inflammatory properties can ease that tightness indirectly by calming systemic inflammation. You might find yourself reclining in the early evening with a cup of licorice tea, the steam carrying the scent of the root into your sinuses, and your diaphragm softly rising and falling with less resistance.

Blue vervain (Verbena hastata) is a less known but potent ally for the nervous system’s role in articulation. It’s a nervine, which means it soothes the nervous system and offers gentle anxiolytic effects. If your vocal cords feel raw from shouting or your voice trembles with unshed tears, blue vervain’s subtle bitterness and floral notes help untangle the knotted fascia around the hyoid bone and thyroid cartilage. This untangling is not metaphorical: fascia, the connective tissue, can hold tension patterns from trauma and repression. Blue vervain’s tincture, taken in small doses before moments of confrontation or self-expression, acts almost like a balm for the vocal apparatus and the mind’s anxious anticipation of speaking out.

This is not about choking down a pill or sugary cough drop. These herbs demand your attention: the slow ritual of preparation, the smell of steeping roots and leaves, the warm liquid sliding past a tight pharynx. They remind you that the body knows—your jaw remembers its clenched past, your diaphragm recalls how to expand, your throat muscles remember how to open. When you name your fury out loud, supported by these herbal allies, you reclaim something sacred: the right to sound fierce, to be heard, without apology or shame.

This is not pathology; this is biology. The body insists on expression. The throat, that vulnerable corridor of voice and breath, asks for care and courage. These herbs are medicine, not magic, and they meet you at the intersection of muscle, fascia, and nervous system. Sip slowly. Speak fiercely. Let the sound of your fury move through you like wind through the trees after a storm, wild and unbroken.

## The Wisdom of the Teachers: Karla McLaren’s Lens on Anger as a Boundary-Restoring Messenger

It’s just after sunset, the sky bruised a deep indigo, and your masseter muscle clenches without your consent, a silent scream trapped behind clenched teeth. The jaw remembers. It remembers the times you swallowed your fury, convinced you’d be less if you let it out. But Karla McLaren, who has taught so many of us to listen deeply to anger, reminds you that this muscle tension is not a failure of will or character. It’s biology. Your anger is a boundary-restoring messenger, a compass pointing out where your skin has been crossed, where your energy field has been invaded.

McLaren’s perspective insists that anger is not the enemy; it’s the herald. It arrives not to make you “bad” or “ugly” but to alert you to a violation. The tight fascia around your trapezius and levator scapulae—the weight you carry there, especially on gray, rain-heavy evenings—holds the memory of these boundary breaches. The body knows this. The diaphragm contracts in a shallow, defensive breath, as if bracing for harm that’s already passed but still echoes in the nervous system. This is not pathology; this is biology’s way of saying, “Enough.”

Articulation—naming the anger out loud—is the muscle memory retraining your nervous system needs. When you say, “I am angry because you ignored my request,” you are not just uttering words. You are shifting the tension out of your neck and jaw, sending a message to your nervous system that this violation will no longer be tolerated. It is a somatic act as much as a verbal one. The masseter unclenches, the diaphragm softens, and the psoas, which often tightens as a protective shell, begins to let go. You are literally setting a boundary with your voice.

McLaren’s work, grounded in empathy and fierce respect for the body’s wisdom, aligns with what Pema Chödrön calls “leaning into the edge”—the moment when you face your discomfort rather than fleeing it. You learn that articulating anger is not about blaming or shaming; it’s about declaring your territory in the landscape of relationships. When you speak your truth, the air around you smells different—more like the sharp, green tang of crushed mugwort or the resinous warmth of pine—herbs that clear stagnant energy and open space for new boundaries to take root.

This practice is an act of self-respect, a reclamation. It’s the difference between the silent tightening of the jaw at 2 a.m., when shadows pool and the body tenses against the night, and the clear, resonant voice that carries your boundary into the world. The wisdom McLaren offers is a reminder: anger is your ally, not your enemy. To articulate it is to honor your body’s truth and to weave protection into the fabric of your daily life. Your voice, steady and unafraid, becomes the living boundary that your muscles have been aching to set all along.

## Somatic Practice: Releasing the Jaw and Throat to Let Sound Flow

It’s 4:37 a.m., the sky a bruise-blue stretching into dawn, and you’re lying on your back, the weight of your body pressing evenly into the mattress. The air is cool, almost sharp against the bare skin of your face. You can taste the faint bitterness of chamomile steeping on the nightstand, mingling with the low hum of your own breath. This moment—the stillness before the sun’s first light—offers a rare invitation to meet the body where it stores what you’ve never said.

Start by bringing your attention to the masseter muscle, that thick band on the sides of your jaw where clenched fury often nests. You may feel it as a tight, stubborn knot pressing behind the angle of your jaw, a clenched fortress guarding words you were told to swallow. Place your fingers gently there, just beneath the cheekbones. The skin is warm, a little taut with tension. Now, slowly open your mouth, allowing the jaw to drop. Not a forced gape, but a soft, deliberate opening that lets the masseter begin to stretch and release.

As you do this, notice the levator scapulae muscles at the back of your neck, those small, deep fibers that lift the scapula and can hold the burden of unspoken anger in a silent shrug. Your neck might feel stiff, a signal that your body remembers every time you “bit your tongue” or “held your peace.” Tilt your head slightly to one side, feeling the stretch along the side of your neck and into the base of your skull. This isn’t about achieving perfect posture; it’s about discovering the places where sound gets stuck between the vertebrae, like a secret waiting to be told.

Next, bring your attention to the throat itself. The fascia here is thin and pliable, but it can become constricted with fear, shame, or habit. Place a hand lightly over your thyroid cartilage—the little bump at the front of your throat, the “Adam’s apple”—and breathe into that space. Inhale slowly through your nose, feeling the diaphragm expand downward, pushing gently into your belly, then exhale with a soft sigh. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your ribs, is the engine of voice, and when it’s free, sound flows effortlessly.

Now, allow a low, guttural hum to rise from deep within your belly. Don’t think about words or meaning. Feel the vibration—how it resonates through your chest, travels up your throat, and causes your jaw to tremble slightly as if the sound is shaking loose years of unspoken fury. Let the hum grow louder, moving from a murmur to a full-bodied sound, an unembarrassed release of energy.

Teachers like Karla McLaren remind us that anger and fury are not enemies to be banished but signals to be heard and expressed. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that the body knows where these emotions live, and the jaw remembers every time it was told to shut. This practice isn’t about performance; it’s about reclaiming your voice from the places it’s been held hostage.

As you finish, close your mouth gently and rest your hands on your chest. Notice the subtle shifts—the slight loosening in your face, the softening of your neck’s edges, the relaxed pulse beneath your fingertips. This is your body speaking out loud, not in words, but in the release of held tension. The sound you made was a bridge between your inner landscape and your outer world, a necessary passage for truth to move through you.

You might feel raw or even vulnerable after this practice, and that’s exactly as it should be. Resmaa Menakem and Bessel van der Kolk remind us that trauma and suppressed emotion are stored in our tissues, not just our minds. What you’ve just done is biology, not pathology—a small but profound act of self-recognition and liberation. When you name your fury out loud, with your body as your guide, you begin to rewrite the story your jaw and throat have been holding for far too long.

## The Aftermath of Speaking: The Body’s Reckoning

You finish speaking, and the silence that follows is not empty. It is full—full of the echoes of your words vibrating in the air and inside your body. Your masseter muscle, which clenched as you found the courage to name your fury, now relaxes in a slow, reluctant release. Your trapezius and levator scapulae, those chronic hoarders of tension, begin to soften, though the ache lingers like an old friend you’re not quite ready to say goodbye to. Speaking your truth is a small battle, and your body remembers the skirmish in the tightness along your jawline and the hollow ache behind your eyes.

The clock reads 9:37 pm; the room is dim, the air thick with summer’s humidity, sticky against your skin. There’s a salty tang—the unmistakable scent of sweat mingled with the faint musk of your own skin’s flora, stirred awake by the sudden surge of adrenaline. This is not shame or embarrassment; this is biology. Your sympathetic nervous system was triggered, flooding your body with energy to fight or flee, and now your parasympathetic system starts its slow, essential work of settling you back down. Your diaphragm, once caught high and tight beneath your ribcage, begins to drop, drawing long, uneven breaths that taste faintly of the late summer air through the cracked window.

You don’t need to rush to “calm down” or “get over it.” As Resmaa Menakem teaches, the body speaks a language older than words. It remembers what the mind tries to forget. The ache in your trapezius and the heaviness in your psoas aren’t failures; they’re the body’s way of saying, “I was here. I held this. I survived.” These sensations are not pathology; they are the somatic imprint of your truth being voiced. You might notice your breathing deepen now, slow like the tide pulling away from the shore, or perhaps it stays jagged and shallow a while longer. Both are valid responses. Your nervous system is sorting itself out, neuron by neuron, muscle fiber by muscle fiber.

There’s a certain relief in this exhaustion—a surrender to the body’s wisdom. You might find yourself leaning against a cool wall, feeling the steady thrum of your heartbeat through the thin fabric of your shirt, smelling the faint trace of crushed lavender from the herbal salve you applied earlier. This is the grounding balm your body craves after the storm of articulation. It is as if the diaphragm’s slow descent massages the solar plexus, untying knots you didn’t know were there. You might catch your reflection in a window, eyes glassy but clear, the corners of your mouth softening. This is the ripple effect of saying what needed to be said. The jaw remembers its release, the breath its freedom, the sweat its purging heat.

In the quiet aftermath, there is a tenderness you owe yourself. Thich Nhat Hanh’s teaching that “the energy of mindfulness is the energy of love” rings true here—not the cloying sugar of sentiment, but the fierce, unyielding kindness of presence. You hold space for the body that just spoke its truth, that just carried the weight of your words. You don’t push the exhaustion away; you lean into it, knowing this is part of the healing. The nervous system’s settling is a slow unfolding, a softening that honors the deep biology of your fury and its release. Your body remembers. Your body knows.

## FAQ: Speaking Your Anger Into the Body

**Q: When I try to say my anger out loud, my throat clamps up and it’s hard to get the words out. Is that normal?**

**A:** Absolutely. The throat is where the masseter and the muscles around the larynx—the cricothyroid and thyrohyoid, for example—tend to tighten when we’re suppressing anger. This isn’t a sign that you’re failing; it’s your body doing exactly what it’s designed to do. The jaw remembers every time you were told to be quiet, to “not be ugly,” to swallow your fury. Thich Nhat Hanh’s teachings on mindful breathing can be helpful here, especially focusing on the breath’s movement through the throat. Try inhaling deeply into your diaphragm, feeling the levator scapulae and trapezius relax as you prepare to speak. Then, as you exhale, let the sound emerge slowly, even if it’s just a hum or a guttural noise at first. This is not about forcing words out but about coaxing the fascia and muscles in your throat to soften and open over time.

**Q: Sometimes when I speak my anger aloud, my body shakes uncontrollably. What’s going on?**

**A:** The shaking is your nervous system’s way of discharging stored tension. Resmaa Menakem talks about this somatic release as the body’s natural method of calming itself after trauma or suppression. When you articulate anger, especially if it’s been buried for years, your diaphragm and psoas muscles might contract and release rapidly, causing tremors. This isn’t a sign of weakness or instability; it’s your biology recalibrating. You might notice your hands clenching or your shoulders twitching as well. Rather than resisting this shaking, try to allow it to happen in a safe space—maybe late at night when the world is quiet and your senses are less bombarded. The body knows how to regulate; your job is to give it permission.

**Q: How do I keep from feeling overwhelmed by the intensity of my anger when I say it aloud?**

**A:** Gabor Maté often emphasizes the importance of boundaries—not just with others but within yourself. When you speak your anger, your diaphragm and intercostal muscles expand and contract with more force, sending signals of urgency to your brain. It’s easy to get swept into a tidal wave of emotion. One method is to ground yourself physically by pressing your feet firmly into the floor or feeling the weight of your sitting bones against a chair. Notice the subtle engagement of your erector spinae muscles along your spine. Naming the sensation helps too: “This is anger in my sternum, this is rage in my jaw.” Labeling turns the experience from a flood into a river you can navigate. You don’t have to stay in the storm forever; you can step back to the shore whenever you need.

**Q: Is it better to say my anger aloud to myself, or to another person?**

**A:** Both have their place, but the body responds differently depending on context. Speaking anger aloud to yourself—whether aloud or in a journal—engages your own auditory and motor cortices without the added stress of another’s gaze or judgment. It’s a rehearsal space, a way to map the contours of your fury in the safe territory of your own voice and body. When you speak to another person, your trapezius and rhomboid muscles might tighten in anticipation of defense or connection, depending on your relationship and history. Trust Robert Augustus Masters’ guidance here: start with your internal voice, then experiment with safe witnesses—someone who can hold your fury without trying to fix or minimize it. This practice reconditions your nervous system to tolerate and ultimately integrate your anger, not just in your throat and jaw, but through your whole fascia network.
